title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1 Introduction
This Quarto book is a review workspace for the USACE Recreation Ontology and its related artifacts. It is designed to help subject matter experts, GIS staff, and ontology reviewers inspect the model structure, visualize relationships, and compare the ontology against supporting spatial and crosswalk materials.

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.1 Purpose
This site supports team review of the recreation ontology by organizing the core materials into a single navigable website.
The review package includes:

an overview of the ontology scope and modeling intent
an interactive visualization of ontology classes and properties
supporting spatial diagrams
the SDSFIE crosswalk material used for comparison and traceability

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.2 How to use this site
Recommended review flow:

Start with the Introduction for context and review goals.
Read the Ontology Overview to understand the model structure and intended interpretation.
Open Ontology Visualization to inspect class hierarchies and property relationships interactively.
Review the Spatial Diagrams for GIS-oriented context.
Review the SDSFIE Crosswalk to compare ontology terms with external structures.

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.3 Included source artifacts
This site is built from the following repository assets:

data/01-recreation-vocabulary.ttl
data/02-recreation-ontology.ttl
data/06-recreation-spatial.ttl
data/12-recreation-sdsfie-crosswalk.ttl

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.4 Reviewer guidance
When reviewing the ontology, focus on questions such as:

Are the class hierarchies intuitive and complete?
Do object properties connect the right kinds of entities?
Do datatype properties capture the operational attributes needed by users?
Are any labels, definitions, or modeling boundaries unclear?
Do the ontology and supporting diagrams align with expected recreation business concepts?

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.5 Notes
This website is intended for collaborative review and discussion. It emphasizes readability and navigation over exhaustive RDF inspection.
Continue to the next chapter to begin the review.

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.1 Scope
The recreation ontology models core recreation business entities, managed places, facilities, amenities, services, programs, fees, and related operational concepts relevant to USACE recreation management.
The primary ontology source for this chapter is:

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text: data/02-recreation-ontology.ttl

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.2 High-level structure
At a high level, the ontology is organized around several major concept areas:

Domain entities that represent the broad recreation business space
Physical and managed assets such as recreation areas, facilities, and amenities
Water-access and linear assets for specialized physical features
Visitor services and programs representing operational and service concepts
Fees, agreements, incidents, and activities representing supporting business concepts

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.3 Class hierarchy patterns
The ontology uses rdfs:subClassOf to establish class hierarchies. These hierarchies help distinguish:

general versus specialized recreation concepts
managed places versus supporting amenities
physical assets versus operational/service concepts

Examples of expected hierarchy patterns include:

broad domain classes that act as abstract parents
facility subclasses for more specialized recreation facilities
amenity subclasses for supporting infrastructure
specialized trail, campground, and aquatic-use concepts

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.4 Property model
The ontology includes both object properties and datatype properties.

2.4.1 Object properties
Object properties connect one resource to another resource. In this ontology, they are used to express relationships such as:

an area having a facility
a facility having an amenity
an asset supporting an activity
an entity being managed under a program

These relationships are especially important for understanding how the model expresses operational structure rather than just classification.


2.4.2 Datatype properties
Datatype properties connect a resource to a literal value such as text, number, date, or decimal. In this ontology, they are used for values such as:

local identifiers
facility names
counts of sites or slips
trail length
operating dates

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.5 External references
The ontology also connects to external namespaces and concepts, including:

SKOS concepts
XML Schema datatypes
OWL classes such as owl:Thing

These references help the ontology align with supporting vocabularies and standard semantic web constructs.

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.6 How to review this ontology
A useful way to review the ontology is to separate the work into three passes:

2.6.1 1. Hierarchy pass
Check whether classes are arranged appropriately and whether subclassing is intuitive.


2.6.2 2. Relationship pass
Review object properties and verify that domains, ranges, and property names make sense.


2.6.3 3. Attribute pass
Review datatype properties and confirm that literal-valued attributes are assigned to the right classes with the right datatypes.

title: 2  Ontology Overview
text:
2.7 Next step
The next chapter provides an interactive graph-based visualization of the ontology to support structural review.

title: 4  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
4.1 Ontology Scope
These diagrams describe the active spatial extension in 06-recreation-spatial.ttl. The spatial ontology models GIS-recordable feature representations; it does not replace recreation business entities, source vocabulary concepts, or geometry instances.
flowchart LR
    RECS["recs: Recreation Spatial Ontology"]
    REC["rec: Recreation Domain Ontology"]
    USF["usf: Spatial Foundation"]
    UPR["upr: Physical Realms"]
    GEO["GeoSPARQL"]

    RECS -->|imports| REC
    RECS -->|imports| USF
    RECS -->|imports| UPR
    RECS -->|imports| GEO
    RECS --> RSF["recs:RecreationSpatialFeature"]

title: 4  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
4.2 Core Spatial Contract





classDiagram
    class GeoFeature
    class SpatialFeature
    class RecreationSpatialFeature {
        <<owl:Class>>
        +sourceLayerName
        +featureGrain
        +sourceIdentifier
    }
    class RecreationDomainEntity
    class SourceConcept
    class Geometry
    class PhysicalRealm
    class SpatialPattern

    GeoFeature <|-- RecreationSpatialFeature
    SpatialFeature <|-- RecreationSpatialFeature
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> RecreationDomainEntity : representsEntity
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> SourceConcept : representsConcept
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> Geometry : geo_hasGeometry
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> PhysicalRealm : intersectsPhysicalRealm
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> SpatialPattern : usesSpatialPattern

title: 4  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
4.14 Property Matrix










Property
Type
Domain
Range
Role




recs:representsEntity
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
rec:RecreationDomainEntity
Links a GIS representation to the managed recreation entity it represents.


recs:representsConcept
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
skos:Concept
Links a GIS representation to a source-backed classification, zoning, or boundary concept.


rec:hasSpatialRepresentation
Object property
rec:RecreationDomainEntity
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
Inverse of recs:representsEntity.


recs:associatedTrail
Object property
recs:TrailAssociatedFeature
rec:Trail
Links a trail-associated point feature to the trail it belongs with without claiming that the point feature is the trail line representation.


rec:hasTrailAssociatedFeature
Object property
rec:Trail
recs:TrailAssociatedFeature
Inverse of recs:associatedTrail.


recs:usesSpatialPattern
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
owl:Thing
Declares whether the feature is modeled as a representation or direct feature pattern.


recs:intersectsPhysicalRealm
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
upr:PhysicalRealm
Connects a spatial feature to land, water, or other physical realm context.


recs:sourceLayerName
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Preserves the source layer or feature class name.


recs:featureGrain
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Explains what one source row or feature represents.


recs:sourceIdentifier
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Preserves the source record identifier or crosswalk key.

title: 5  Recreation Modernization Decision Log
text:
Topic
Decision
Rationale




RecreationFacility
Preserve with narrower role
Business/domain role for managed recreation facilities; not a spatial feature or geometry.


RecreationAmenity
Preserve with clearer role
Supporting asset role associated with facilities; amenities may have their own spatial representations.


BoatRamp
Split entity and feature
rec:BoatRamp is the managed asset; recs:BoatRampFeature is the GIS representation.


RecreationArea class A/B/C
Reclassify
Treat as source-backed classification concepts unless implementation requires OWL classes.


CampAreaClass A/B/C/D/E
Reclassify
Treat as fee/development classification concepts rather than subclasses of Campground.


LandClassification and LandAllocation
Reclassify
Preserve as SKOS classification values linked from recreation areas.


Trail
Preserve with spatial representation
rec:Trail is a managed linear recreation asset; recs:TrailFeature is the line representation.


Trail-associated point features
Add generic point-feature pattern
recs:TrailAssociatedFeature captures point features linked to a trail and typed by source or standard concepts; recs:TrailheadFeature is the explicit trailhead class. This avoids collapsing trail line representations and trail-associated point records into one class family.


Feature terminology
Retain established naming and document dual use
Feature may mean a modeled notable element or class name inherited from source/community practice, and it may also mean a GIS feature or spatial record. Do not rename solely to remove the ambiguity; disambiguate with explicit definitions, notes, representation metadata, and role concepts.


Marina
Preserve and facet
Business facility and water access asset; spatial representation may include multiple geometry records.


FuelDock and generic dock semantics
Defer broad generalization
Generic dock semantics require physical-realm and implementation review before broad inheritance.


Legacy schema fields
Use as migration evidence
Existing fields inform crosswalks and implementation profiles but do not define target ontology semantics.


Spatial extension
Rebuild
Spatial features must use explicit representation relationships, not only skos:related.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.1 Purpose
Status: v2 review draft Date: 2026-06-17 Sources: Recreation v2 artifacts; SDSFIE 5.0 Gold Draft vocabulary, ontology, and enumerations
This is the operator-facing crosswalk for teams that start with a SDSFIE geospatial database and need to map records into the recreation ontology.
Use the tables below as: “If you have this SDSFIE class or coded value, use this recreation ontology term.”
The companion file 12-recreation-sdsfie-crosswalk.ttl remains the machine-readable assertion layer. It still records the formal SKOS predicates from recreation terms to SDSFIE anchors.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.2 Scope Boundary
The recreation ontology is derived first from formal USACE guidance, especially EP 1130-2-550. SDSFIE alignment is secondary.
That means this crosswalk is meant to help with mapping from existing SDSFIE data into the current recreation ontology. It is not a rule that every SDSFIE recreation family must become a USACE recreation class.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.3 How To Use This Crosswalk

Start with the most specific SDSFIE class or coded value available in the source database.
Prefer the listed recs:*Feature class for the spatial record.
When an optional rec:* concept is listed, use it to type what the feature represents.
If the row says Review, do not auto-map it. Keep the source term visible and resolve it in an implementation profile or data-cleanup pass.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.4 Match Labels







Label
Meaning for the team




Default
Use this recreation term unless the source record clearly says otherwise.


Conditional
Use this term only when the source semantics match the note in the row.


Review
No single safe default target exists in the current ontology.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.5 Direct SDSFIE Feature-Class Mappings










If your SDSFIE source is
Use this recreation spatial class
Optional recreation concept
Match
Notes




sd:Campground
recs:CampgroundFeature

Default
Direct campground-to-campground mapping.


sd:Campsite
recs:CampsiteFeature

Default
Direct campsite-to-campsite mapping.


sd:RecreationTrail
recs:TrailFeature
rec:Trail or a narrower trail concept
Default
Use the trail line class. Do not confuse this with point-based trail-associated features.


sd:RecreationTrailFeature
recs:TrailAssociatedFeature

Default
Use for point features associated with a trail.


sd:ParkingArea
recs:ParkingAreaFeature

Default
Direct parking-area mapping.


sd:Marina
recs:MarinaFeature

Conditional
Use when the source record is functioning as a recreation marina, not just a general harbor facility.


sd:RecreationBoundary
recs:RecreationAreaFeature
rec:RecreationArea
Conditional
Use when the SDSFIE feature is the boundary of a recreation area. Do not use this as the default for recs:ProjectBoundaryFeature.


sd:Playground
recs:RecreationAmenityFeature
rec:Playground
Conditional
The ontology keeps playground primarily as an amenity concept rather than a dedicated spatial subclass.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.6 SDSFIE Recreation Feature Type Mappings
Use these rows when the source record is stored as a generic SDSFIE recreation feature and the coded value carries the real meaning.










If your SDSFIE source is
Use this recreation spatial class
Optional recreation concept
Match
Notes




sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_boatRamp
recs:BoatRampFeature

Default
This is the correct recreational boat-ramp path. Do not remap these to sd:BoatRamp.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_picnicSite
recs:PicnicAreaFeature

Default
SDSFIE stores picnic-site meaning in the coded value rather than a standalone class.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_restroom
recs:RestroomFeature
rec:FlushRestroom
Default
SDSFIE restroom is flush-water oriented in this crosswalk.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_vaultToilet
recs:RestroomFeature
rec:VaultToilet
Default
Use the restroom feature class and type the represented concept as vault toilet.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_museumOrVisitorCenter
recs:VisitorCenterFeature

Conditional
Use when the source record is functioning as a visitor center. If the source is really a museum, review first.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_swimmingDesignatedBeach
recs:SwimmingAreaFeature

Conditional
Good fit when the source is a designated swimming beach or area.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_waterActivitySite
recs:SwimmingAreaFeature

Conditional
Use only when the source is specifically a swimming area. Otherwise review.


sd:RecreationFeature + sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_picnicShelter
recs:RecreationAmenityFeature
rec:PicnicShelter
Conditional
The current ontology has the picnic shelter concept, but not a dedicated picnic-shelter spatial subclass.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.7 SDSFIE Trail Use Value Mappings
Use these rows when the source feature is sd:RecreationTrail and the trail-use value further narrows the represented concept.










If your SDSFIE source is
Use this recreation spatial class
Optional recreation concept
Match
Notes




sd:RecreationTrail + sde:RecreationTrailUseType_hike
recs:TrailFeature
rec:HikingTrail
Conditional
Keep the spatial class as trail feature and carry the hiking semantics in the represented concept.


sd:RecreationTrail + sde:RecreationTrailUseType_bike
recs:TrailFeature
rec:BikeTrail
Conditional
Keep the spatial class as trail feature and carry the bike semantics in the represented concept.


sd:RecreationTrail + sde:RecreationTrailUseType_equestrianOrPack
recs:TrailFeature
rec:EquestrianTrail
Conditional
SDSFIE combines equestrian and pack use under one value.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
section: 6.8 SDSFIE Sources That Need Review Instead Of Auto-Mapping
text:
6.8 SDSFIE Sources That Need Review Instead Of Auto-Mapping








SDSFIE source
Why it needs review
Current recreation guidance




sd:RecreationFeature with no useful type value
Too generic to choose among facility, amenity, water-access, or area patterns.
Review the source semantics before choosing recs:RecreationFacilityFeature, recs:RecreationAmenityFeature, recs:WaterAccessFeature, or another class.


sd:BoatRamp
SDSFIE scopes this class to non-recreational ramps.
For recreational ramps, use sd:RecreationFeature with sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_boatRamp, then map to recs:BoatRampFeature.


sd:FishingFeature
Broader than the current fishing-pier concept.
Map to recs:FishingPierFeature only when the source record is specifically a fishing pier or platform.


sd:DockOrWharf
Can represent several different recreation meanings.
Review against recs:FuelDockFeature, recs:FishingPierFeature, rec:BoatSlip, or rec:PumpOutStation based on source semantics.


sd:LandUse
Related, but not equivalent, to USACE recreation land classification.
Use only as reviewed support for recs:LandClassificationAreaFeature.


sd:RestrictedArea
Only some water-surface zones are actually restrictive zones.
Use only as reviewed support for recs:WaterSurfaceZoneFeature.


sd:GolfCourseFeature, sd:HuntingFeature, and similar SDSFIE recreation families outside current USACE formal-guidance scope
These are part of broader SDSFIE recreation coverage, but not default USACE recreation ontology targets.
Do not add or auto-map these into the current USACE recreation ontology without separate formal-guidance support and review.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.9 Implementation Rule
Use this markdown as the quick operator lookup. Use 12-recreation-sdsfie-crosswalk.ttl when you need the formal reviewed SKOS assertions behind it.

title: 7  Recreation Vocabulary Review
text:
7.1 How to use this page
This page is designed to support review of the controlled vocabulary that informs the recreation ontology.
It focuses on:
Recommended review sequence:
As you review, consider:

title: 7  Recreation Vocabulary Review
text:
Start with the Vocabulary In Context diagram
Review the Major Vocabulary Families
Use the Interactive Vocabulary Explorer to inspect specific concepts
Consult the Concept Inventory for a tabular audit view



Are these items really best modeled as concepts rather than classes?
Are broader/narrower relationships intuitive?
Are any concepts too implementation-specific for a shared vocabulary?
Are concept-to-ontology links clear and appropriate?

title: 7  Recreation Vocabulary Review
text:
7.5 Review Notes
Interpret the graph with the following distinctions in mind:

Concept Scheme nodes represent organizing schemes or collections
Top Concept nodes usually act as major entry points in the vocabulary
Grouped concept nodes are heuristically clustered for reviewer readability
Ontology Link Target nodes are not vocabulary concepts; they are linked resources from the ontology or related model artifacts

This page is intentionally optimized for review clarity rather than full RDF completeness.

title: 7  Recreation Vocabulary Review
text:
7.7 Implementation Notes
This scaffold keeps the processing functions inside the .qmd for now so the page is immediately runnable.
Recommended next refactor steps:

Move RDF parsing helpers into R/io_ttl.R
Move SKOS extraction into R/parse_skos.R
Move graph-building functions into R/build_visnetwork.R
Replace inline functions with sourced helpers or an internal package

title: 7  Recreation Vocabulary Review
text:
7.8 Assumptions and updates
This page assumes the collaborator-supplied vocabulary file is Turtle and primarily uses SKOS-style constructs.
If collaborators update the vocabulary regularly, the page should continue to work as long as the file remains RDF/Turtle and still exposes at least some of:

skos:Concept
skos:ConceptScheme
skos:prefLabel
skos:broader
skos:inScheme

If your collaborator file uses different predicates or naming conventions, the constants section near the top is the main place to adapt it.
